items.myth: KL divergence is a distance metric between probability distributions.
items.reality: KL is a divergence: non-negative and zero iff P = Q, but it is asymmetric and does not satisfy the triangle inequality, so it is not a metric.
items.example: You can have distributions where D_KL(P||Q) is small but D_KL(Q||P) is large — impossible for a true distance.

items.myth: Smaller cross-entropy always means smaller KL to the true data distribution.
items.reality: H(P, Q) = H(P) + D_KL(P || Q). If P itself changes (different label noise or class balance), H(P) shifts and cross-entropy comparisons across datasets can mislead.
items.example: Balanced vs imbalanced label sets change H(P); compare KL or model log-loss on the same held-out P for fair model comparison.

items.myth: KL is always finite for any two distributions you can sample from.
items.reality: If Q assigns zero probability to an outcome that P can produce, D_KL(P || Q) = ∞ regardless of how well Q fits other outcomes.
items.example: A generative model Q never predicting a valid rare token that P assigns positive mass makes KL infinite in theory.
